parts.heading: Introduction
parts.text: The As-Is Process area documents how the Bank Guarantee Issuance process (BGIT) runs today at Deutsche Bank for corporate clients. A bank guarantee is an irrevocable commitment by the bank to a third-party beneficiary on a corporate client's behalf, making it a high-value trade-finance instrument where errors carry regulatory, financial, and reputational consequence. The area is documented across seven sections — an overview, six roles, seven process steps, two exceptions, three pain points, four metrics, and five open process gaps — and provides the foundational baseline from which the transformation programme will be designed. At approximately 180 guarantees issued per month, growing at 8% year-on-year and peaking roughly 40% above the monthly mean in November and December, the process volume is material and seasonal capacity pressure is a structural concern the documentation explicitly records.

parts.heading: Current state
parts.text:
The six roles in the process are cleanly partitioned by function. The Trade Finance Officer (ROLE-BGIT-001) carries the broadest accountability, holding the Responsible assignment across five of the seven process steps — application intake, credit and facility check, wording review, guarantee generation and delivery, and collateral confirmation. The Trade Finance Manager (ROLE-BGIT-002) owns issuance approval, and the Head of Trade Finance (ROLE-BGIT-003) provides additional sign-off authority for larger or partially-secured guarantees. The Compliance Analyst (ROLE-BGIT-004) owns sanctions screening, the Credit team (ROLE-BGIT-005) adjudicates facility limit decisions when headroom is insufficient, and the Legal team (ROLE-BGIT-006) reviews and approves bespoke wording. RACI coverage is broadly consistent, with every step carrying at least one Responsible assignment.

The seven process steps follow a logical arc from application receipt to SWIFT delivery. Application Intake (PS-BGIT-001) opens the process with the Trade Finance Officer checking six required data fields through the Corporate Portal, producing a validated application record. The Credit and Facility Check (PS-BGIT-002) verifies that the client holds sufficient approved facility headroom; if not, the application is parked and routed to the Credit team, which may grant a temporary one-off uplift, initiate a permanent facility increase as a separate process, or decline — triggering a formal rejection issued to the client through the portal. Wording Review (PS-BGIT-003) is a deliberate branch in the flow: standard-wording applications require no Trade Finance Officer action and proceed immediately, while bespoke-wording applications are emailed to the Legal team for review and sign-off before advancing. Sanctions and Compliance Screening (PS-BGIT-004) follows, with the Compliance Analyst screening the beneficiary and country against the sanctions list using the Sanctions Screening Tool; a hit suspends the application pending investigation. The flow then forks based on collateral type: fully-secured applications proceed directly to Issuance Approval (PS-BGIT-005), while partially-secured guarantees pass through Collateral Confirmation (PS-BGIT-007), a mandatory step requiring the Trade Finance Officer to verify that cash collateral has been received and blocked before approval is sought. Issuance Approval requires the Trade Finance Manager's review and sign-off recorded in the Trade Finance System, with the Head of Trade Finance providing additional sign-off for larger or collateral-backed guarantees. Finally, Guarantee Generation and Delivery (PS-BGIT-006) produces the executed instrument in the Trade Finance System, dispatches it to the beneficiary's bank via SWIFT, and updates the client's facility utilisation.

The two documented exceptions both carry HIGH impact. An Insufficient Facility Limit (EX-BGIT-001) is confirmed as the most common source of delay, handled by the Credit team, with no formal SLA governing the response window. A Sanctions Screening Hit (EX-BGIT-002) suspends the application pending Compliance investigation; no frequency data has been elicited for this exception, leaving its operational significance unquantified.

Three pain points are recorded, all categorised as delay. Credit Limit Stalls (PP-BGIT-001) is rated HIGH severity: clients submit applications without pre-arranging sufficient facility limit, triggering a Credit team referral whose informal 1–2 business-day response window is neither published nor enforced, compounding the stall duration. Bespoke Wording Delays (PP-BGIT-002) carry MEDIUM severity: the absence of a committed Legal review turnaround makes end-to-end cycle time unpredictable for bespoke-wording applications. No SLA for Incomplete Application Follow-up (PP-BGIT-003) is also MEDIUM: incomplete applications returned through the portal carry no formal deadline for the client to respond and no escalation trigger for the Trade Finance Officer, meaning cycle-time stalls can begin before the issuance workflow has started.

Four metrics quantify performance. Monthly issuance volume (M-BGIT-002) stands at approximately 180 guarantees per month with an improving trend and an 8% year-on-year growth rate; the seasonal peak implies the process must achieve its targets at volumes of 250 or more guarantees per month, not just the base load. The straight-through processing rate (M-BGIT-003) is the headline efficiency indicator, measured at approximately 52% against a 70% target, with the 48% of applications requiring at least one manual touchpoint concentrated at intake completeness and credit pre-check. The exception escalation rate (M-BGIT-004) is approximately 22% against a target below 15%, and has been flat for twelve months, suggesting its drivers are structural rather than volume-dependent. End-to-end turnaround (M-BGIT-001) carries a stated target of 5 business days in the metric element, but the pain-point elements PP-BGIT-001, the process gap PG-BGIT-001, and PG-BGIT-003 all reference a 3-business-day client commitment — an internal inconsistency that leaves the operative SLA ambiguous. No measured actual turnaround figure exists anywhere in the wiki today.

Five process gaps are documented, all open. PG-BGIT-001 captures the missing Credit team response SLA, which is the direct structural cause of PP-BGIT-001. PG-BGIT-002 documents that the bespoke wording handoff to Legal is email-only, with no system workflow, no automatic routing, and no record in the Trade Finance System. PG-BGIT-003 records the absence of a committed Legal review SLA. PG-BGIT-005 adds the compliance-facing dimension: with no system workflow, there is no audit trail capturing when the request was sent, when Legal responded, or what wording was reviewed, a weakness also flagged in audit finding OAF-BGIT-002. PG-BGIT-004, the fifth gap, is the most foundational measurement problem: the process has no end-to-end cycle-time metric anchored to application receipt and SWIFT dispatch timestamps, meaning the bank cannot confirm automatically whether the client commitment is being met.

parts.heading: What stands out
parts.text:
The strength of this As-Is documentation is its concrete specificity. The seven process steps are fully populated — transitions mapped, roles assigned, and provenance grounded in SME elicitation for most elements — and the four performance metrics provide a quantitative baseline that is unusually complete for a process at this documentation stage. The confirmed STP rate of 52% versus a 70% target and the flat 22% exception escalation rate give the transformation team directly actionable numbers.

Two structural weaknesses stand out. First, the Legal handoff is the single most documented vulnerability in the process, yet it is fragmented across three overlapping gap entries — PG-BGIT-002, PG-BGIT-003, and PG-BGIT-005 — that address the same root problem from different angles: off-system routing, no committed turnaround, and no audit trail. Together they reveal a compliance-documentation exposure that extends well beyond the operational delay captured in PP-BGIT-002, but the overlapping coverage makes it harder to assess remediation scope clearly. Second, two internal inconsistencies undermine the reliability of the as-is baseline as a measurement anchor. The end-to-end SLA target conflicts across elements — 5 business days in M-BGIT-001 versus a 3-business-day commitment cited in three other elements. The Head of Trade Finance sign-off threshold also conflicts: the index and PS-BGIT-005's Outputs section state EUR 5 million, while the What happens body of PS-BGIT-005 and ROLE-BGIT-003 both state EUR 2 million. Neither inconsistency has been surfaced or resolved in the existing provenance record. Separately, ROLE-BGIT-003 carries proposed-source provenance across both of its template sections, meaning the Head of Trade Finance role content has not yet been confirmed by the SME, despite that role being the designated process owner in the index. EX-BGIT-002 also lacks any frequency data, leaving the sanctions screening hit exception — whose handling can delay issuance indefinitely — with no quantitative characterisation.

parts.heading: Recommendation
parts.text: Three actions should precede any further wiki elaboration. The end-to-end SLA target inconsistency — 3 versus 5 business days — and the Head of Trade Finance sign-off threshold inconsistency — EUR 2 million versus EUR 5 million — should both be resolved with the SME in the next session and corrected in the affected elements before they propagate into the target design. These are not minor editorial issues; they define the performance bar the transformation is measured against and the approval authority structure. Second, the three Legal-handoff gap entries (PG-BGIT-002, PG-BGIT-003, PG-BGIT-005) should be rationalised into a single consolidated gap element that covers the system workflow gap, the missing SLA, and the missing audit trail in one coherent statement, reducing the wiki's redundancy and making the remediation path unambiguous. Third, ROLE-BGIT-003 should be brought to elicited provenance through targeted SME questioning — as the process owner and the holder of the Accountable role at the highest-risk approval step, its content cannot remain at proposed status.
